name: Phase 2b: Batch Constraint
summary:
After the GKR phase reduces the logUp claim to an evaluation claim at a random point xi, the Batch Constraint module takes over. It must verify that every present AIR's numerator/denominator contribution at xi is consistent with the stated constraints and interactions. This is done by batching across AIRs (with challenge mu), batching across constraints within an AIR (with lambda), batching across interaction message components (with beta_logup), running a sumcheck, and finally evaluating each AIR's symbolic constraint DAG at the opening point.

Thirteen AIRs cooperate: FractionsFolderAir receives the per-AIR claims from GKR and samples mu; UnivariateSumcheckAir and MultilinearSumcheckAir run the sumcheck protocol; InteractionsFoldingAir and ConstraintsFoldingAir sample their respective challenges and fold per-interaction/per-constraint evaluations; a cluster of Eq AIRs (Eq3bAir, EqNsAir, EqUniAir, EqSharpUniAir, EqNegAir) precomputes multilinear eq polynomial values used as weights; SymbolicExpressionAir walks every AIR's constraint DAG node-by-node; and ExpressionClaimAir aggregates all per-trace (num, denom) claims into the single value that the sumcheck pipeline must equal.

blocks.html:
The verifier reaches BC with a pair of running claims (p_xi_claim, q_xi_claim) produced by GKR, along with the opening point xi and the logUp batching challenge alpha_logup. BC's job is to prove that these claims are what you would get if you (a) opened every column of every present AIR at xi, (b) plugged those openings into each AIR's symbolic constraints and interactions, and (c) summed everything weighted by appropriate Lagrange / eq-polynomial factors. The protocol does this soundly via several layers of random batching plus a two-stage sumcheck (one univariate round that skips l_skip variables, then n_max multilinear rounds).

We'll follow the verifier's own order: first the handoff from GKR (FractionsFolderAir), then the batching samples (beta, lambda), then the sumcheck itself, then the eq-polynomial subsystem, and finally DAG evaluation plus the closing claim-aggregation step that ties everything together.

blocks.heading: FractionsFolderAir
blocks.html: This AIR is the handoff point from GKR. For each present AIR it observes the pair (numerator_term[i], denominator_term[i]) on the transcript, aggregates them into (cur_p_sum, cur_q_sum), samples the fraction-batching challenge mu, and hashes the two aggregates into a single sum_claim that becomes the input to the sumcheck. It also forces the row count to equal num_present_airs (handed in from ProofShapeAir).

blocks.heading: Cols: per-row state
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy, Debug)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct FractionsFolderCols<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,
    pub is_first: T,

    pub air_idx: T,

    pub tidx: T,

    pub sum_claim_p: [T; D_EF],
    pub sum_claim_q: [T; D_EF],
    pub cur_p_sum: [T; D_EF],
    pub cur_q_sum: [T; D_EF],
    pub mu: [T; D_EF],
    pub cur_hash: [T; D_EF],
}
blocks.explain: Each row represents one present AIR. sum_claim_p / sum_claim_q are the per-AIR numerator/denominator observed on the transcript; cur_p_sum / cur_q_sum accumulate them; mu is the batching challenge broadcast to all downstream AIRs; cur_hash is the Horner-style fold that becomes the sumcheck input.

blocks.heading: Decrement air_idx row-by-row; force it to end at 0
blocks.explain: air_idx starts at num_present_airs - 1 on the first row and decrements by 1 per row, ending at 0 on the last row. Combined with the FractionFolderInputBus handoff (below), this forces the row count to exactly equal the number of present AIRs — the verifier cannot silently skip an AIR's contribution.

blocks.heading: Accumulate per-AIR p and q into running sums
blocks.explain: The final cur_p_sum / cur_q_sum on the last row must equal the GKR outputs p_xi_claim / q_xi_claim — the check is closed out by the gkr_claim_bus.receive at the end of this AIR. Soundness here is: each AIR's contribution appears in the transcript and shows up in the running sum; the identity p_xi == 0 and q_xi == alpha_logup is enforced elsewhere by whoever sent the original claims on BatchConstraintModuleBus.

blocks.heading: Horner hash: sum_claim = p + mu * q, folded across AIRs
blocks.explain: This is the algebraic heart of the fraction-folding step: every pair (sum_claim_p[i], sum_claim_q[i]) is interleaved into a single polynomial sum_k mu^k * x_k using Horner's method with two terms per AIR (numerator then denominator). After the last row, cur_hash is the single extension-field value that the sumcheck will have to match.

blocks.heading: Receive num_present_airs, sample mu, observe (p, q)
blocks.explain: The transcript absorbs every (p, q) pair first, then samples mu at the tidx just after the last observation. Because each row decrements tidx by 2 * D_EF (see the transition constraint on line 119), the tidxes of all observations are deterministic, non-overlapping, and pinned to the transcript state.

blocks.heading: Send sum_claim to sumcheck; close GKR handoff; broadcast mu
blocks.explain: Three handoffs close out the AIR: (1) cur_hash is sent to UnivariateSumcheckAir as the initial sumcheck claim (round -1); (2) the gkr_claim_bus receive verifies that the totals equal what GKR output; (3) mu is broadcast on BatchConstraintConductorBus so ExpressionClaimAir can use the same value when it folds in the other direction at the end.

blocks.html: Before sumcheck can run, BC also samples two more batching challenges: beta_logup (for interactions) and lambda (for constraints). These are both drawn from the transcript before the mu sample conceptually — they're interleaved into GKR's transcript range using tidx handoffs from GkrInputAir. The transcript's per-tidx ownership ensures no collision.

blocks.heading: InteractionsFoldingAir
blocks.html: This AIR has two jobs. Early on it samples beta_logup — the challenge used to fold each interaction's multi-field message into a single extension-field value count for the numerator and sum_k beta^k * msg_k + (bus_idx + 1) * beta^{len} for the denominator. Later on (row-by-row, after column openings are available via SymbolicExpressionAir), it computes those folded values and accumulates them per-AIR, weighted by eq_3b, ready to be sent to ExpressionClaimAir.

blocks.heading: Cols: interaction-folding state
blocks.explain: Rows are a three-level nested loop: (proof_idx, sort_idx, interaction_idx), with each interaction spanning multiple rows for its count, bus_idx, and per-message-component values. cur_sum runs beta-Horner within a single interaction; final_acc_num / final_acc_denom accumulate cur_sum * eq_3b across all interactions inside an AIR.

blocks.heading: Sample beta_logup on the first row of each proof
blocks.explain: beta_tidx is handed in from GkrInputAir through InteractionsFoldingInputBus. The tidx lives inside GKR's transcript range (interleaved at tidx_if) but because TranscriptBus enforces per-tidx ownership, the sample is bound to a specific sponge state. The second / third air_shape_bus lookups are what forces the row count inside each AIR to equal its declared NumInteractions.

blocks.heading: Beta-fold: cur_sum = value + beta * next.cur_sum
blocks.explain: The denominator side of each interaction is sum_k beta^k * msg_k + (bus_idx + 1) * beta^{msg_len}. This is evaluated by Horner iteration backwards through the message rows: on the last row, cur_sum = value (the bus index, shifted); on each preceding row, cur_sum = value + beta * next.cur_sum. The numerator (is_first_in_message) is just value untouched — it's the count.

blocks.heading: Accumulate folded (num, denom) per AIR, multiplied by eq_3b
blocks.explain: Once cur_sum is the folded numerator (at is_first_in_message) or folded denominator (at is_second_in_message), it is multiplied by eq_3b (the multilinear eq polynomial evaluated at this interaction's stacked index, supplied by Eq3bAir) and accumulated into final_acc_num / final_acc_denom. These running sums hold all the interactions of one AIR combined.

blocks.heading: Send per-AIR (num, denom) claims to ExpressionClaimAir
blocks.explain: On the first row of each AIR, the completed (final_acc_num, final_acc_denom) pair is sent to ExpressionClaimAir at indices (2 * sort_idx, 2 * sort_idx + 1) — matching the interleave scheme that ExpressionClaimAir uses when folding with mu.

blocks.heading: Receive per-interaction values from SymbolicExpressionAir
blocks.explain: The actual value columns aren't computed here: they come from SymbolicExpressionAir, which evaluates each interaction's count, msg_k, and bus_idx node from the DAG. This is a permutation bus: each (air_idx, interaction_idx, idx_in_message) must match exactly one DAG evaluation. The three branches distinguish multiplicity (numerator), message components (denominator), and bus index (shifted by 1 because (bus_idx + 1) prevents collision with empty messages).

blocks.heading: Receive eq_3b from Eq3bAir on the per-interaction message start
blocks.explain: Exactly one eq_3b value per interaction is pulled from Eq3bAir, on the is_first_in_message row. All other rows within the interaction inherit it via the is_same_message consistency constraint above.

blocks.heading: ConstraintsFoldingAir
blocks.html: Symmetric to InteractionsFoldingAir but for constraints. It samples lambda (the constraint-batching challenge), receives one evaluation per constraint from SymbolicExpressionAir, folds them via Horner as sum_j lambda^j * constraint_j, multiplies by the AIR's eq_n factor (the multilinear eq evaluation at the opening point), and sends one (idx = sort_idx, value) per AIR to ExpressionClaimAir.

blocks.heading: Cols: constraint-folding state
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Copy, Clone)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct ConstraintsFoldingCols<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub is_first: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,

    pub air_idx: T,
    pub sort_idx: T,
    pub constraint_idx: T,
    pub n_lift: T,

    pub lambda_tidx: T,
    pub lambda: [T; D_EF],

    pub value: [T; D_EF],
    pub cur_sum: [T; D_EF],
    pub eq_n: [T; D_EF],

    pub is_first_in_air: T,
}
blocks.explain: A simpler two-level loop than InteractionsFoldingAir: (proof_idx, sort_idx) with constraint_idx iterating within each AIR. n_lift is the AIR's lift amount (inherited from NLiftBus) used to look up the correct eq_n.

blocks.heading: Sample lambda on the first row of each proof
blocks.explain: Same pattern as beta_logup: the tidx is handed in from GkrInputAir, and the sample is bound to the transcript sponge state at that position.

blocks.heading: Horner-fold constraints and multiply by eq_n
blocks.explain: The first-in-air row holds cur_sum = sum_j lambda^j * constraint_j after rolling through all constraints via Horner. AIRs with zero constraints are handled by the last assertion: their single row holds value = 0.

blocks.heading: Send batched (eq_n * folded_sum) per-AIR to ExpressionClaimAir
blocks.explain: The folded_sum trick handles the edge case where an AIR has no constraints (the row is the only one, so is_same_air = 0 and we fall back to local.cur_sum = local.value = 0). Multiplying by eq_n weights the claim by the outer eq polynomial before sending it to ExpressionClaimAir at index sort_idx.

blocks.heading: Look up eq_n by (n_lift, is_sharp=0)
blocks.explain: EqNsAir publishes all eq_n values on EqNOuterBus keyed by (is_sharp, n). Here we look up the non-sharp variant at n = n_lift. For interactions, ExpressionClaimAir will use the sharp variant on the same bus.

blocks.html: With beta, lambda, and mu all sampled, and the handoff from GKR sealed, the sumcheck proper begins. It has two stages: one univariate round that handles the l_skip skipped variables all at once over the roots-of-unity domain, then n_max standard multilinear rounds.

blocks.heading: UnivariateSumcheckAir
blocks.html: The first sumcheck round is run over the roots-of-unity domain of size 2^l_skip (where l_skip is the number of variables skipped at the front — chosen to make this round efficient). The prover sends a univariate polynomial of degree univariate_deg = (max_constraint_degree + 1) * (2^l_skip - 1) as a list of coefficients; the verifier observes each, checks the domain-sum identity |D| * (a_0 + a_{|D|}) == sum_claim, samples r_0, and evaluates the polynomial at r_0 via Horner.

blocks.heading: Cols: univariate sumcheck state
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy, Debug)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct UnivariateSumcheckCols<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,
    pub is_first: T,

    pub coeff_idx: T,

    /// Powers of generator of order 2^{l_skip} to get periodic selector columns
    pub omega_skip_power: T,
    pub is_omega_skip_power_equal_to_one: T,
    pub is_omega_skip_power_equal_to_one_aux: IsEqualAuxCols<T>,

    pub coeff: [T; D_EF],
    pub sum_at_roots: [T; D_EF],

    pub r: [T; D_EF],
    pub value_at_r: [T; D_EF],

    pub tidx: T,
}
blocks.explain: One row per coefficient, iterating coeff_idx from univariate_deg down to 0. omega_skip_power cycles through powers of the domain generator; when it equals 1, the current coefficient lies on the domain and contributes |D| * coeff to sum_at_roots. value_at_r runs Horner evaluation at r_0.

blocks.heading: Force exactly univariate_deg + 1 coefficients
blocks.explain: The row count is VK-determined: the prover cannot submit more or fewer coefficients than the declared degree allows. This is a key soundness property — if the prover could truncate, they could hide a nonzero high-degree term.

blocks.heading: Sum-over-roots check: accumulate |D| * coeff at domain points
blocks.explain: For a polynomial with coefficients a_0, a_1, ..., a_n and domain of size |D|, the sum over x in D equals |D| * sum_{i : i ≡ 0 mod |D|} a_i. That's the identity being checked: accumulate |D| * coeff exactly when omega_skip_power = 1.

blocks.heading: Horner evaluation at r_0
blocks.explain: Standard Horner: a_n + r*(a_{n-1} + r*(a_{n-2} + ...)). Because coeff_idx decreases from n to 0, the first row's coeff is a_n and the last row's coeff is a_0.

blocks.heading: Observe coefficients, sample r_0, check domain identity, hand off
blocks.explain: The claim_bus.receive with round = -1 is what pins sum_at_roots to equal cur_hash from FractionsFolderAir — i.e. |D| * (a_0 + a_|D|) == sum_claim. The claim_bus.send with round = 0 hands value_at_r to MultilinearSumcheckAir as the starting claim for its first multilinear round.

blocks.heading: Broadcast r_0 on ConstraintSumcheckRandomnessBus and BatchConstraintConductorBus
blocks.explain: The challenge r_0 is sent to two buses: ConstraintSumcheckRandomnessBus (consumed by Stacking at the end) and BatchConstraintConductorBus as R[0] (consumed by the Eq AIRs, which need r_0 alongside the other r_js). The * TWO multiplicity is because it is looked up by both EqUniAir and other places.

blocks.heading: MultilinearSumcheckAir
blocks.html: After the univariate round, we run n_max standard multilinear sumcheck rounds. Each round: the prover sends a degree-s_deg = max_constraint_degree + 1 polynomial as s_deg + 1 point evaluations s(0), s(1), ..., s(s_deg); the verifier observes them, checks s(0) + s(1) == current_claim, samples r_j, and computes the next claim as s(r_j) via Lagrange interpolation.

blocks.heading: Cols: multilinear sumcheck state
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy, Debug)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct MultilinearSumcheckCols<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,
    pub round_idx: T,
    pub is_proof_start: T,
    pub is_first_eval: T,

    /// A valid row which is not involved in any interactions
    /// but should satisfy air constraints
    pub is_dummy: T,

    pub eval_idx: T,

    pub cur_sum: [T; D_EF],
    pub eval: [T; D_EF],

    pub prefix_product: [T; D_EF],
    pub suffix_product: [T; D_EF],
    // 1 / i!(d - i)!
    pub denom_inv: T,

    // Lagrange coefficients for the interpolated polynomial
    // prefix_product * suffix_product * denom_inv
    pub lagrange_coeff: [T; D_EF],

    pub r: [T; D_EF],

    pub tidx: T,
}
blocks.explain: Rows are a nested loop over (proof_idx, round_idx, eval_idx), with eval_idx running 0..=s_deg per round. prefix_product, suffix_product, and denom_inv together give the Lagrange coefficient for interpolating at r_j.

blocks.heading: Maintain prefix/suffix products and denom_inv (Lagrange coefficients)
blocks.explain: For evaluating s(r) from values s(0), s(1), ..., s(d), the standard Lagrange formula is s(r) = sum_i s(i) * prod_{j != i} (r - j)/(i - j). Here, prefix_product = prod_{j < i} (r - j), suffix_product = prod_{j > i} (r - j) * (-1)^{d - i}, and denom_inv = 1 / (i! * (d - i)!). Together they give the Lagrange coefficient without ever needing division in-circuit.

blocks.heading: Accumulate s(r_j) = sum_i s(i) * lagrange_coeff(i)
blocks.explain: On the last eval_idx of a round, cur_sum equals s(r_j) — the new claim for round j + 1.

blocks.heading: Per-round: observe evals, check s(0)+s(1)=claim, sample r_j, send next claim
blocks.explain: On the first eval_idx of each round, claim_bus.receive asserts s(0) + s(1) == current_claim. On the last eval_idx, the sampled r_j is locked in and cur_sum = s(r_j) is sent as the next claim. The round_idx chain closes on ExpressionClaimAir's num_multilinear_sumcheck_rounds = n_max, forcing the number of rounds.

blocks.heading: Broadcast r_j to downstream modules
blocks.explain: Each r_j is sent on ConstraintSumcheckRandomnessBus (consumed by Stacking's OpeningClaimsAir to check that the column openings match this sumcheck point) and on BatchConstraintConductorBus as R[round_idx + 1] (consumed by the Eq AIRs). Indices 1..=n_max are all distinct within one proof.

blocks.html: Parallel to the sumcheck, a cluster of Eq AIRs precomputes the multilinear equality polynomial evaluations that the folding AIRs need. The key building blocks are: EqUniAir computes eval_eq_uni(l_skip, xi[0], r_0) — the "base factor" that covers the skipped dimensions. EqSharpUniAir/EqSharpUniReceiverAir compute eval_eq_sharp_uni — the logUp-specific variant over roots of unity. EqNsAir multiplies these with per-dimension factors eval_eq_mle([xi[l_skip + j - 1]], [r_j]). Eq3bAir handles the stacked-b-index bit lookup for interactions. And EqNegAir handles the edge case where log_height < l_skip (negative dimension count).

blocks.heading: Eq3bAir
blocks.html: For each interaction in each AIR, Eq3bAir computes eq_3b_per_trace[i][interaction_idx] = eval_eq_mle(xi[l_skip + n_lift..l_skip + n_logup], b), where b is the interaction's stacked-b index restored bit by bit. This weighs each interaction by its position in the logUp virtual-row numbering.

blocks.heading: Cols: per-bit eq product state
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct Eq3bColumns<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub is_first: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,

    pub sort_idx: T,
    pub interaction_idx: T,

    pub n_lift: T,
    pub n_logup: T,
    pub two_to_the_n_lift: T,
    pub n: T,
    pub hypercube_volume: T, // 2^n
    pub n_at_least_n_lift: T,

    pub has_no_interactions: T,

    pub is_first_in_air: T,
    pub is_first_in_interaction: T,

    pub idx: T,         // stacked_idx >> l_skip, restored bit by bit
    pub running_idx: T, // the current stacked_idx >> l_skip
    pub nth_bit: T,

    pub xi: [T; D_EF],
    pub eq: [T; D_EF],
}
blocks.explain: Three-level loop (proof_idx, sort_idx, interaction_idx), with n iterating through bits of the stacked-b index inside each interaction. Only bits at positions [n_lift, n_logup) contribute — below n_lift they're padded zero, above n_logup the AIR doesn't have that many dimensions.

blocks.heading: Per-bit eq multiplication
blocks.explain: This is the classic multilinear-eq recurrence: eq(x, y) = prod_i (x_i * y_i + (1 - x_i) * (1 - y_i)). Here x_i = xi[l_skip + n] (a field element) and y_i = nth_bit (a boolean). The factor is xi * nth_bit + (1 - xi) * (1 - nth_bit).

blocks.heading: Send eq_3b at last bit of each interaction
blocks.explain: xi[l_skip + n] is looked up on BatchConstraintConductorBus at each bit that actually contributes. The final eq is sent to InteractionsFoldingAir on eq_3b_bus. eq_3b_shape_bus forces (n_lift, n_logup, num_interactions) to match the AIR's VK.

blocks.heading: EqNsAir
blocks.html: Multiplies per-dimension factors to produce eq_ns[n] and eq_sharp_ns[n] for each n. These are looked up by ConstraintsFoldingAir (is_sharp = 0) and ExpressionClaimAir (is_sharp = 1). Each row corresponds to one n value.

blocks.heading: Cols: per-n eq accumulator
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct EqNsColumns<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub is_first: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,

    pub n: T,
    pub n_logup: T,
    pub n_max: T,
    pub n_less_than_n_logup: T,
    pub n_less_than_n_max: T,
    pub is_transition_and_n_less_than_n_max: T,
    pub xi_n: [T; D_EF],
    pub r_n: [T; D_EF],
    pub r_product: [T; D_EF],
    pub r_pref_product: [T; D_EF],
    pub one_minus_r_pref_prod: [T; D_EF],
    pub eq: [T; D_EF],
    pub eq_sharp: [T; D_EF],

    /// The number of traces whose `n_lift` equals `local.n`.
    /// Note that it cannot be derived from `xi_mult` because
    /// `xi_mult` counts interactions, not AIRs with interactions.
    pub num_traces: T,
    pub xi_mult: T,
    pub sel_first_count: T,
    pub sel_last_and_trans_count: T,
}
blocks.explain: Each row fixes one n. xi_n = xi[l_skip + n], r_n = r[n + 1]. eq and eq_sharp accumulate the per-dimension product; they differ only in their seed (from EqUniAir vs. EqSharpUniReceiverAir on EqZeroNBus).

blocks.heading: Per-row eq update: multiply both eq and eq_sharp by (1 - xi - r + 2*xi*r)
blocks.explain: The multiplier is 1 - (xi + r - 2*xi*r) = xi*r + (1-xi)*(1-r) — the standard eq factor. Both eq and eq_sharp accumulate it identically; they differ only in initial conditions (supplied on EqZeroNBus).

blocks.heading: Publish eq_n values on EqNOuterBus (keyed by is_sharp, n)
blocks.explain: The published value is eq[n] * r_product, where r_product = prod_{k >= n+1} r[k] — the "front-loaded" correction that folds in the tail of r. The multiplicity is num_traces (for the is_sharp=0 variant, consumed by ConstraintsFolding) or 2 * num_traces (for the sharp variant, consumed twice by ExpressionClaim, once per num+denom).

blocks.heading: Receive initial eq/eq_sharp seeds from EqUniAir / EqSharpUniReceiverAir
blocks.explain: On the first row, eq = eval_eq_uni(l_skip, xi[0], r_0) (from EqUniAir) and eq_sharp = eval_eq_sharp_uni(...) (from EqSharpUniReceiverAir). EqZeroNBus is the permutation handoff.

blocks.heading: EqUniAir
blocks.html: Computes the base factor eval_eq_uni(l_skip, xi[0], r_0) / 2^l_skip iteratively by squaring x and y at each step and updating res = (x + y) * res + (1 - x)(1 - y). This is exactly the iterative form of eval_eq_uni over l_skip steps.

blocks.heading: Cols and iterative recurrence
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct EqUniCols<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub is_first: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,

    pub x: [T; D_EF],
    pub y: [T; D_EF],
    pub res: [T; D_EF],
    pub idx: T,
}
blocks.explain: x starts at xi[0], y at r_0. Each row squares both and multiplies the running res. After l_skip steps, res / 2^l_skip is sent on EqZeroNBus as the non-sharp seed for EqNsAir.

blocks.heading: Recurrence: square x, y; update res
blocks.explain: The eval_eq_uni function computes prod_{i < l_skip} (1 - x^{2^i} - y^{2^i} + 2*x^{2^i}*y^{2^i}) / 2. Rewriting as (x+y)*res + (1-x)(1-y) with res being the running product gives the recurrence used here. On the first row res = 1; on the last (idx == l_skip) res is the full product.

blocks.heading: EqSharpUniAir
blocks.html: The sharp variant of EqUniAir: computes eval_eq_sharp_uni(xi[0..l_skip], r_0) over roots of unity. This is more involved than EqUniAir because it iterates over nested rounds, each corresponding to successive halvings of the unit-root order, and uses a bus (EqSharpUniBus) to express the recursion algebraically across rows.

blocks.heading: Cols: per-iteration state (xi_idx, iter_idx, roots, running product)
blocks.code:
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Clone, Copy)]
#[repr(C)]
pub struct EqSharpUniCols<T> {
    pub is_valid: T,
    pub is_first: T,
    pub proof_idx: T,

    pub root: T,
    pub root_pow: T,
    pub root_half_order: T,

    pub xi_idx: T,
    pub xi: [T; D_EF],
    pub product_before: [T; D_EF],

    pub iter_idx: T,
    pub is_first_iter: T,
}
blocks.explain: xi_idx iterates from l_skip - 1 down to 0 (the variable being folded in this "round"). iter_idx runs through the current root_half_order iterations within the round. At the final row, root must equal canonical_inverse_generator — enforcing the correct size of the final domain.

blocks.heading: Root squaring: every time iter_idx wraps, root^2 and order doubles
blocks.explain: Start with root = -1, root_half_order = 1 (a 2-element domain: {1, -1}). Each wrap squares root and doubles the order, doubling the effective domain size. By iteration l_skip, the domain has 2^{l_skip} elements.

blocks.heading: Per-iteration: split product into (1 - xi + xi*root_pow) and (1 - xi - xi*root_pow) halves
blocks.explain: EqSharpUniBus is a self-permutation bus: each row receives product_before at its current position and sends two products forward — one at the same iter_idx, one at iter_idx + root_half_order. The two sends use (1 - xi + xi*root_pow) and (1 - xi - xi*root_pow) respectively, implementing the sharp-eq tree split. The final (received on the receiver AIR) collects all 2^{l_skip} leaves.

blocks.heading: EqSharpUniReceiverAir
blocks.html: Collector for EqSharpUniAir: Horner-combines the 2^{l_skip} sharp-uni leaves with r_0 and divides by 2^{l_skip} to produce the initial eq_sharp seed for EqNsAir.

blocks.heading: Receive leaves, Horner-fold with r_0, send seed on EqZeroNBus
blocks.explain: The Horner recurrence cur_sum = coeff + r * next.cur_sum produces sum_i coeff_i * r_0^i over all 2^{l_skip} leaves. Dividing by 2^{l_skip} gives the final eq_sharp_0 seed.

blocks.heading: EqNegAir
blocks.html: Handles AIRs whose log_height < l_skip (so n = log_height - l_skip < 0). In this regime the eq and selector polynomials cannot be computed by the standard EqUniAir/EqSharpUniAir pipeline and require a separate iterative scheme over r^{2^|n|}.

blocks.heading: Cols: per-(neg_hypercube, row_index) state
blocks.code:
#[repr(C)]
#[derive(AlignedBorrow, Debug)]
pub struct EqNegCols<F> {
    // Proof index columns for continuations
    pub proof_idx: F,
    pub is_valid: F,
    pub is_first: F,
    pub is_last: F,

    // Negative hypercube of the current section + section row index
    pub neg_hypercube: F,
    pub neg_hypercube_nz_inv: F,
    pub row_index: F,
    pub is_first_hypercube: F,
    pub is_last_hypercube: F,

    // Value of u^{2^row}, r'^{2^row}, and (r' * omega)^{2^row}, where
    // r' = r^{2^neg_hypercube}
    pub u_pow: [F; D_EF],
    pub r_pow: [F; D_EF],
    pub r_omega_pow: [F; D_EF],

    // Running product of (u^{2^i} + r'^{2^i}) from i to row_index
    pub prod_u_r: [F; D_EF],
    pub prod_u_r_omega: [F; D_EF],
    pub prod_1_r: [F; D_EF],
    pub prod_1_r_omega: [F; D_EF],
    pub sel_first_count: F,
    pub sel_last_trans_count: F,
    pub one_half_pow: F,
}
blocks.explain: neg_hypercube indexes the size of the negative-dimension hypercube; within each section, row_index iterates. u_pow / r_pow are the squaring chains; the four prod_* columns accumulate running products used to build eq_n(u, r) and the rotation selector k_rot_n(u, r).

blocks.heading: Squaring chains for u_pow, r_pow, r_omega_pow
blocks.explain: At each row, u_pow and r_pow square, and the running products prod_u_r = prod_i (u^{2^i} + r^{2^i}) / prod_1_r = prod_i (1 + r^{2^i}) accumulate. These are the factors needed for the negative-dimension eq polynomial.

blocks.heading: Send eq and rotation selector to EqNegResultBus
blocks.explain: The closed-form eq = prod_u_r - u^{2^|n|} + 1 uses the invariant prod_i (u^{2^i} + r^{2^i}) - u^{2^|n|} + 1 = eq_|n|(u, r). Same for k_rot with r * omega.

blocks.html: With all the eq values precomputed, we can now tackle the core of BC: evaluating each AIR's symbolic constraint DAG at the opening point, feeding those values into the folding AIRs, and finally checking the aggregated claim against the sumcheck output.

blocks.heading: SymbolicExpressionAir
blocks.html: Walks every present AIR's symbolic expression DAG node-by-node. Each row evaluates one node: constants, variables (columns or public values), selectors (is_first/is_last/is_transition), arithmetic (Add, Sub, Mul, Neg), and interaction markers (InteractionMult, InteractionMsgComp, InteractionBusIndex). Values are looked up and sent on SymbolicExpressionBus (a self-permutation bus keyed by (air_idx, node_idx)). When a root node is reached — a constraint or an interaction component — the value is forwarded to ConstraintsFoldingAir or InteractionsFoldingAir.

blocks.heading: NodeKind: the DAG opcode set
blocks.explain: 14 opcodes. Each NodeKind is selected by the flags encoder (degree-2 over NUM_FLAGS = 4), so at most one opcode is active per row.

blocks.heading: Cached (VK-pinned) columns vs per-proof single-main columns
blocks.explain: The DAG structure (flags, attrs, fanout, constraint_idx) is fixed by the VK and lives in a cached trace. The dynamic data (slot_state, args, sort_idx, n_abs) is per-proof and lives in the common main.

blocks.heading: Per-opcode evaluation: build value from args based on selector
blocks.explain: The loop computes value as sum_k sel_k * expr_k where at most one sel_k is 1. For variables, the args are the witnessed column opening (provided on ColumnClaimsBus). For arithmetic, args are sub-DAG results looked up on SymbolicExpressionBus. For selectors, the two args are sel_uni (base-field selector from SelUniBus) and the eq prefix (SelHypercubeBus), multiplied to produce is_first/is_last.

blocks.heading: Publish value on SymbolicExpressionBus; lookup child args
blocks.explain: Each node's value is published on SymbolicExpressionBus with multiplicity fanout (how many parent nodes reference this one). Child args for Add/Mul/etc. are looked up from the same bus. Because it's a permutation bus, the prover cannot fabricate intermediate values — every reference must match a publication.

blocks.heading: Resolve variables: lookup column openings on ColumnClaimsBus
blocks.explain: ColumnClaimsBus carries the transcript-observed column openings from Stacking's OpeningClaimsAir. PublicValuesBus carries the public values declared by each AIR. Both are permutation buses, pinning variable values to what was observed.

blocks.heading: Forward interaction nodes to InteractionsFoldingAir
blocks.explain: Root nodes are forwarded out: InteractionMult/InteractionMsgComp nodes go to InteractionsFoldingAir; InteractionBusIndex nodes also go there (with idx_in_message = -1); constraint roots (with is_constraint = 1) go to ConstraintsFoldingAir. These are exactly the messages whose receives we saw earlier in those two AIRs.

blocks.heading: ExpressionClaimAir
blocks.html: The closing AIR of BC. For each present AIR it receives (num, denom) from InteractionsFoldingAir (two claims per AIR) and value from ConstraintsFoldingAir (one claim per AIR). It folds them all with mu (using the same interleave scheme as FractionsFolderAir), multiplies each by a multiplier that encodes eq_sharp_ns plus a norm factor for negative-dimension AIRs, and on the first row of each proof checks that the aggregate equals the sumcheck's final claim. This is the closing equality that makes BC sound.

blocks.heading: Cols: (proof, group, trace, parity) nested indexing
blocks.explain: For t present AIRs there are 2t interaction rows (num/denom per trace) followed by t constraint rows, giving 3t rows per proof. idx_parity alternates 0/1 on interaction rows. The doc-comment in the source shows the exact row layout example.

blocks.heading: Compute multiplier: eq_sharp_ns or eq_sharp_ns / 2^|n| depending on sign
blocks.explain: Constraint rows use multiplier = 1 (the eq_n factor is already baked in by ConstraintsFoldingAir). For interactions: positive-n rows use multiplier = eq_sharp_ns; numerator rows on negative-n AIRs multiply by 2^{|n|} to undo the norm_factor = 1 / 2^{|n|} applied during the sumcheck domain. The denominator uses eq_sharp_ns unconditionally.

blocks.heading: Mu-fold: cur_sum = value * multiplier + next_cur_sum * mu
blocks.explain: The fold interleaves all 3t (value * multiplier) terms via Horner, yielding evaluated_claim = sum_k mu^k * (value_k * multiplier_k). The order matches the order FractionsFolderAir used when it built sum_claim.

blocks.heading: Close the loop: check evaluated_claim == final sumcheck claim
blocks.explain: The sumcheck_claim_bus.receive at round = n_max is BC's closing equality: MultilinearSumcheckAir sends the final claim from its last round, and ExpressionClaimAir receives it here as the expected value of cur_sum. If these don't match, the permutation bus balance fails. The expression_claim_n_max_bus.receive forces num_multilinear_sumcheck_rounds to equal the sumcheck's declared round count, and the expr_claim_bus.receive closes the permutation with InteractionsFoldingAir and ConstraintsFoldingAir.

blocks.heading: Look up eq_sharp_ns and check power consistency via PowerCheckerBus
blocks.explain: hyperdim_bus forces (n_abs, n_sign) to match each trace's declared log_height. eq_n_outer_bus pulls the sharp eq value — and only at n = n_abs * (1 - n_sign), i.e. zero for negative-dimension AIRs (where the eq is handled separately by EqNegAir). pow_checker_bus verifies n_abs_pow == 2^{n_abs}, needed for the negative-dimension norm factor above.

blocks.html:
At this point every constraint inside BC has closed out via permutation bus balances: the sumcheck's output claim equals the aggregated (num, denom) claim, which is exactly what the verifier would compute if they had every column opening and every constraint evaluation. Sumcheck soundness plus the random batching with beta, lambda, and mu completes the argument.

The remaining outputs of BC — the sumcheck point (r_0, r_1, ..., r_{n_max}) and the final tidx — flow to the Stacking module via ConstraintSumcheckRandomnessBus and StackingModuleBus. Stacking's OpeningClaimsAir will use them to check that the transcript-observed column openings are consistent with the commitment at that point.
